input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising motors, gearboxes, encoders, and a controller. This system is suitable for digital twinning due to its complex mechanical structure, precise motion control requirements, and the need for real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance in industrial manufacturing environments.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tools include SysML for systems engineering modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector perspectives, MathWorks Simulink for simulation, general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java), and methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT for handling complexity. These tools are used to describe system structure, behavior, and integrate heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin integrates computational models using Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies such as SysML for structural and functional architecture across requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical abstraction levels. Behavior is modeled via state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton, while analytical simulations leverage MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, and MontiArc define component & connector relationships, interfaces, and compositions. Data management involves integrating heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and multi-tool environments (SysML, Simulink) to maintain consistency across models.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time-series sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol from physical components to the digital shadow; unidirectional data flow as per digital shadow reference model (Section II.B).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocol: MQTT for sensor data streaming; no details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development process involves specific steps including Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification, as detailed in Section II.C 'How to create Digital Shadows' and Section IV.A 'Methodology' of the source document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support capabilities include identifying friction to improve robot control accuracy and utilizing integrated visualization within a unified dashboard for user interaction.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to industry standards such as SysML [17] for model-based systems engineering, ACME [23], AADL [24], and MontiArc [25], [26] for architecture description languages (ADLs) to define component interfaces and interconnections. Methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are applied across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical). Behavior modeling integrates Simulink [28], simulation tools [29], [30], and general-purpose languages (C++, Java), with interoperability ensured through heterogeneous stakeholder integration [31].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system as a 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and includes specific components like motors, gearboxes, encoders, and a controller, which are explicitly mentioned in the source. The source describes the 'MABI MAX 100 with six axes' (Sec. III), its 'functional reach of 2 m' and 'path repeatability (0.06 mm)' (Sec. III), and details about the motor, gearboxes, encoders, and controller (Sec. III). The extraction also accurately notes the system's suitability for digital twinning due to its complex structure and the need for real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance, which aligns with the paper's focus on improving motion accuracy through digital shadows. However, the extraction could be expanded with more specific details from the text, such as the robot's specific controller (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl) and its use in machining tasks.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is accurate given the specific terminology used in the source. Section II.B explicitly distinguishes between a 'Digital Shadow' and a 'Digital Twin', stating: 'A digital shadow is characterized by an automated one-way data flow... A digital shadow functions as a passive entity... A Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components'. The paper describes the capabilities of the Digital Shadow (visualization in a dashboard, identification of joint friction) but does not describe specific services of the Digital Twin, which would involve active control or feedback.

The extraction is highly specific and strongly grounded in Section II.A of the source text. It correctly lists tools such as 'SysML' for systems engineering modeling across 'requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer' abstraction levels. It accurately identifies methodologies like 'RFLP..., OOSEM..., SPES..., or SMArDT' used for handling complexity. It also lists architecture description languages (ADLs) like 'ACME..., AADL..., MontiArc... or SysML IBDs' for 'component & connector' perspectives, and tools like 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'general-purpose languages like C++ or Java' for behavior description and simulation.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source documents mention 'real-time' data-driven representations and 'live-data linking', but they do not provide specific details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic. The specific temporal aspects of the twinning relationship are not detailed.

The text describes the creation of a digital shadow for 'the first axis of the robot' and focuses on a single instance. It does not mention multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins.

While the document presents a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' and describes component integration (e.g., AAS, SysML), it focuses on the data structure and behavior of a single digital shadow. It does not describe the overall orchestration, resource management, or system-wide coordination mechanisms of a broader Digital Twin system constellation.

The document describes data transmission via 'MQTT' and the use of 'Asset Administration Shells' for the robot's internal data flow and dashboard visualization. It does not discuss integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability with other Digital Twins, or horizontal dependencies.

It is strongly grounded in the text. Relevant quotes: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.' and 'A digital shadow is characterized by “an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object”'.

The document explicitly defines the system as a 'digital shadow' rather than a fully interactive digital twin. Section II.B states that a digital shadow is characterized by 'an automated one-way data flow' and 'functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.' Thus, control flows from digital to physical are not implemented or described.

The extraction correctly identifies the only mentioned protocol (MQTT) for data streaming and accurately notes the absence of other technical details such as network topology, bandwidth, or security in the provided text. Relevant quote: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.'

The source text discusses modeling tools like MathWorks MATLAB Simulink and SysML, as well as Asset Administration Shells (AAS), but it does not specify the hosting infrastructure (e.g., cloud, edge, on-premise), computational requirements, or scalability mechanisms. Consequently, there is no evidence to describe the hosting deployment.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The provided text focuses on digital shadows, friction models, and robot motion control using systems like SysML and Simulink. It does not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, or consent management. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is accurate.

While the document mentions a 'safety cell' restricting the robot's working range, it does not describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or threat mitigation strategies as defined in the characteristic description. The text is entirely focused on the engineering and modeling aspects of the digital shadow.
